name: Cistercian Route: Montblanc - Conesa Loop
name_original: Ruta del Cister – circular desde Montblanc por Conesa
description: Cycle the difficult 38.5-mile Cister Route from Montblanc via Conesa, exploring vineyards, historic villages, and scenic views.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Montblanc, Catalonia, Spain, offers routes through a landscape defined by the Prades Mountains, ancient forests, and vineyards. The terrain features narrow, winding roads that connect medieval towns with natural areas. Cyclists can explore historical sites and rural communities, encountering varied elevation changes across the region. Best touring cycling routes around Montblanc The most popular touring cycling route is Cistercian Route: Montblanc and Poblet Loop, a 19.7 miles (31.7 km) moderate trail that takes about 2 hours 20 minutes to complete. This route connects the historic town of Montblanc with the UNESCO World Heritage site of Poblet Monastery. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Cistercian Route: Montblanc - Conesa Loop, a difficult 38.5 miles (61.9 km) path. This longer route explores more of the region's rural landscapes and historical connections. Local touring cyclists also love the Cistercian Route: Montblanc and Vallbona de les Monges Loop, a 31.8 miles (51.1 km) trail leading through historical Cistercian sites and rural scenery, often completed in about 3 hours 28 minutes. Touring cycling around Montblanc is defined by medieval towns, the Prades Mountains, and vineyard-covered hills. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to more challenging routes with significant elevation gain. The routes in Montblanc are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 300 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Montblanc's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Montblanc?
A: Montblanc offers a diverse selection of over 30 touring cycling routes. These routes cater to various skill levels, with a significant number of moderate and difficult options to explore the region's unique landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Montblanc?
A: The touring cycling routes around Montblanc, Catalonia, Spain, traverse a landscape characterized by the Prades Mountains, ancient forests, and vineyards. You'll encounter narrow, winding roads, often with varied elevation changes, connecting medieval towns with natural areas. The Prades Mountains, in particular, offer rides through rocky outcrops and stunning scenery.

Q: Are there routes suitable for beginners or families in Montblanc?
A: While there are no routes specifically classified as 'easy' for touring cycling, Montblanc does offer 19 'moderate' routes. These can be suitable for cyclists with some experience or families looking for a manageable challenge, allowing them to enjoy the scenic and historical surroundings without extreme difficulty.

Q: What historical sites or natural attractions can I visit along the cycling routes?
A: Many routes in Montblanc offer access to significant historical and natural attractions. You can cycle to the UNESCO World Heritage site of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/840435" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Poblet Monastery&lt;/a&gt;, explore the medieval town of Montblanc itself, or discover tiny villages like La Cadeneta. Natural highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/265083" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cave of the Cranes&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/725611" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Els Avencs de la Febró&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the Montblanc area?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes around Montblanc are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974865331/cistercian-route-montblanc-and-poblet-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cistercian Route: Montblanc and Poblet Loop&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e975277386/cistercian-route-montblanc-conesa-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cistercian Route: Montblanc - Conesa Loop&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e975280616/cistercian-route-montblanc-and-vallbona-de-les-monges-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cistercian Route: Montblanc and Vallbona de les Monges Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: When is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Montblanc?
A: The region around Montblanc, Catalonia, generally offers pleasant cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn are often ideal, with milder temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summers can be warm, especially in July and August, while winters are generally mild but can have cooler, wetter days.

Q: Where can I find parking for touring cycling routes in Montblanc?
A: Montblanc, being a historic town, offers various parking options. You can typically find public parking areas within or on the outskirts of the town, which serve as convenient starting points for many of the circular touring cycling routes. Specific parking details may vary by route, so checking local information upon arrival is recommended.

Q: What are some of the more challenging touring cycling routes in Montblanc?
A: For experienced touring cyclists seeking a challenge, Montblanc offers 19 difficult routes. Notable challenging options include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e975277386/cistercian-route-montblanc-conesa-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cistercian Route: Montblanc - Conesa Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 61.9 km with significant elevation gain, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40363501" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Prades Historic Center – Old Bridge of Montblanc loop&lt;/a&gt;, featuring over 940 meters of ascent.

Q: What do other cyclists say about touring cycling in Montblanc?
A: The touring cycling routes in Montblanc are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars from more than 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the blend of historical exploration, the beauty of the Prades Mountains, and the serene rides through vineyards and ancient forests.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through vineyards or ancient forests?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in Montblanc immerse you in the region's natural and cultivated beauty. You'll find paths that traverse the vineyards of Languedoc and wind through ancient forests, offering a picturesque and varied cycling experience.

Q: Can I find places to eat or rest along the touring cycling routes?
A: The routes often pass through Montblanc itself and other small, authentic villages like La Cadeneta, Farena, and El Pinetell. These settlements provide opportunities to find local cafes, restaurants, or shops where you can rest, refuel, and experience local culture and cuisine.

Q: Are there specific natural monuments worth seeing while cycling?
A: Beyond the general scenic beauty, you can encounter specific natural monuments. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4748206" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Deport Spring&lt;/a&gt; is a notable natural monument, and the region also features summits like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/597960" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puig d'en Cama (717 m)&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/632147" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tossal de la Baltasana summit&lt;/a&gt;, offering impressive views.

Q: What is the longest touring cycling route available in Montblanc?
A: One of the longer touring cycling routes is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e975277386/cistercian-route-montblanc-conesa-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cistercian Route: Montblanc - Conesa Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which spans approximately 61.9 km (38.5 miles). This difficult route allows for an extensive exploration of the region's rural landscapes and historical connections.
tips: Old medieval bridge that crosses the Francolí River on its way through Montblanc.
Medieval building converted into a farmhouse. The castle still retains some old elements, although the multiple transformations it has undergone are also visible.
A charming medieval walled village on a hill. It is listed as a Cultural Property of National Interest by the Generalitat de Catalunya.

Perched on the Serra del Tallat, the town of Forès is located at a very strategic point, dominating the Conca de Barberà region. From its viewpoint you can see both the same region and neighboring Sagarra and if the day is clear, even Montserrat, Montseny and the Mediterranean Sea.

This monument is located next to the Poblet Monastery. Milmanda's name means small castle. Milmanda Castle, an artistic and historical fortification where Emperors Carlos V and Felipe II hunted, can be visited from Monday to Sunday by booking in advance through its website. The tour includes a visit to the vineyard, in the Milmanda Castle, tasting of two wines, and an expert guide or sommelier throughout the tour.

There is news of this fountain as early as 1151, but in its current form it dates from 1852, although the original project was never completed. The stone used in its construction came from an old obelisk that was in the wall of the old road from Poblet to Vimbodí. The number of pipes was also increased from the current 11 to 17 (one pipe for each letter of the town where it is located). Originally the pipes were made of bronze, but they were stolen in 1954 and replaced by iron ones.

faqs.question: How long does it take to complete this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: This touring bicycle route, covering approximately 61.9 km, typically takes around 4 hours and 5 minutes to complete. This duration is for active cycling and does not include stops for sightseeing or rest.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is officially rated as difficult for touring bicycles. While some climbing is gradual, there are sections that can be strenuous, especially given the varied terrain. It's best suited for cyclists with good fitness levels.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Cister Route from Montblanc via Conesa?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied. Approximately 40% of the route is paved (asphalt), but a significant portion (around 59%) consists of undefined surfaces, which may include gravel or well-maintained dirt tracks common in agricultural areas. There are also small segments of unpaved surfaces. Expect a mix of road types, from main roads near Montblanc to quieter, 'outstanding' smaller roads with minimal traffic.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience a pleasurable scenic ride through beautiful countryside. The route features numerous vineyards and olive trees, agricultural landscapes, and charming medieval towns. It also ventures into natural areas like the Poblet Natural Park, offering a blend of rural beauty and historical charm.

faqs.question: What are the key landmarks or points of interest on this route?
faqs.answer: The route passes by several notable points of interest. You can see the Old Bridge of Montblanc, Barberà Castle, the Conesa Medieval Walled Village, and Milmanda Castle. There's also the Viewpoint of the Conca de Barberà offering panoramic views, and the Font Baixa.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this route in Montblanc?
faqs.answer: Montblanc is a convenient starting point due to its good transport links. While specific parking areas aren't detailed, as a historic town, Montblanc will have designated parking facilities. It's advisable to check local municipal websites or parking apps for the most current information on parking options in the town center or near the route's initial access points.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: To enjoy the best conditions, consider cycling during spring or autumn. These seasons offer milder temperatures, which are ideal for cycling, and you can avoid the intense heat of summer. The landscapes are also particularly beautiful during these times, with vineyards and olive groves in full splendor or harvest.

faqs.question: Is this touring bicycle route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' rating and varied terrain, including some undefined surfaces and gradual climbs, this route is generally not recommended for beginners or young families. It's better suited for touring cyclists with a good level of fitness and some experience with longer, more challenging rides.

faqs.question: Is the Cister Route - circular from Montblanc via Conesa dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route largely traverses rural and agricultural areas, specific regulations for dogs on the cycling path itself are not explicitly stated. If cycling with a dog, ensure it is well-behaved, on a leash, and that you carry waste bags. Always be mindful of local wildlife and agricultural land. It's also important to check if any specific attractions or accommodations along the route have their own pet policies.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route, especially within Paraje Natural de Poblet?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are generally required to cycle the GR 175 Cister Route itself, nor for accessing the natural areas like the Paraje Natural de Poblet through which it passes. However, if you plan to visit specific Cistercian monasteries or other historical sites along the way, they may have their own entrance fees. Always respect local regulations, especially concerning protected natural spaces.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route is part of the larger GR 175 Cister Route and also intersects with other paths. You will find sections that overlap with the Ruta del Císter, GR-175 BTT, L'Espluga de Francolí-Vimbodí, and the Camí de Sant Jaume de Tarragona a Lleida - Etapa 4, among others.

waypoints.name: Viewpoint of the Conca de Barberà
